coredata.dc:title: Endogenous NO-release multi-responsive hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles for drug encapsulation and delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Shu
Shi, Jinjing
Chen, Xia
Bai, Lu
Wu, Qiuhua
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: Novel multi-responsive drug delivery vehicles (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs) were prepared by grafting amino-terminated poly (N-vinyl caprolactam) (PNVCL-NH2) and amino-rich carbon dots (CDs) on the surface of aldehyde-functionalized HMSNs (HMSNs-CHO) via Schiff base reaction. The CDs were prepared from L-arginine and their surfaces were rich in guanidine. Doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded into the nanoparticles to form drug loaded vehicles (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX) and the drug loading efficiency was 58.38%. The drug release behaviors of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX showed temperature and pH responsiveness due to the poly (N-vinyl caprolactam) (PNVCL) and Schiff base bond. The high concentration of NO released in high concentration H2O2 of tumor site could induce tumor cells apoptosis. The multi-responsive CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs are intriguing drug carriers, which combine drug delivery and NO release in one.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Drug delivery
Carbon dots
Multi-responsive
Endogenous NO-release

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cellular uptake and toxicity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China
Start-up Funds for Doctoral Research of Liaoning University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJC201906 ) and Start-up Funds for Doctoral Research of Liaoning University ( a280008030 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation procedures and functioning mechanism of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX.
SEM (A) and TEM images (B) of HMSNs; TEM images (C) and DLS measurement (D) of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs.
In vitro release profiles of DOX from CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX at different temperature (pH = 7.4) (A) and pH values (T = 37 °C) (B).
Release profiles of NO from CDs in different concentrations of H2O2 aqueous solution (pH=6.5) (A) and at different pH values (The concentration of H2O2 was 12.5 mmol L−1) (B).
Intracellular NO level in HeLa cells after incubating with CDs (A), CDs@HMSNs (B) and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs (C) for 4 h (green: NO; blue: CDs).
Inverted fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX for 2, 6, and 12 h, respectively (blue: DAPI; red: DOX; green: NO).

MTT cytotoxicity assay of PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX for 48 h in (A) A549, (B) HeLa; Fluorescence images of Calcein-AM (green)/PI (red) co-staining cells (HeLa) with PBS (a), PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX (b) and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX (c) (C), Apoptotic cells are stained red; MTT cytotoxicity assay of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX for 48 h in the presence of catalase in (D) A549, (E) HeLa. Data are represented as means ± SD (n = 3), where *p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001 are considered statistically significant.

head.ce:title: Endogenous NO-release multi-responsive hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles for drug encapsulation and delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110036, P. R. China. Fax: 86-24-62202380.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel multi-responsive drug delivery vehicles (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs) were prepared by grafting amino-terminated poly (N-vinyl caprolactam) (PNVCL-NH2) and amino-rich carbon dots (CDs) on the surface of aldehyde-functionalized HMSNs (HMSNs-CHO) via Schiff base reaction. The CDs were prepared from L-arginine and their surfaces were rich in guanidine. Doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded into the nanoparticles to form drug loaded vehicles (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX) and the drug loading efficiency was 58.38%. The drug release behaviors of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX showed temperature and pH responsiveness due to the poly (N-vinyl caprolactam) (PNVCL) and Schiff base bond. The high concentration of NO released in high concentration H2O2 of tumor site could induce tumor cells apoptosis. The multi-responsive CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs are intriguing drug carriers, which combine drug delivery and NO release in one.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Drug delivery systems (DDSs) can improve the bioavailability of drugs, prolong the circulation time in the bloodstream and abate side effects [1–8]. Commonly used DDSs include polymer micelles, liposomes, dendrimers, carbon nanotubes and silicon nanoparticles. Among them, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) are promising and efficient DDSs due to their large drug loading cavities and permeable mesoporous shell structures [9–13]. Unfortunately, the application of bare HMSNs is limited due to their poor solubility in blood and body fluids, easy absorption by macrophages, and unintelligent release behaviors [14]. Surface modification with water-soluble particles, which are called capping agents [15–22], is the most commonly used method to solve the above problems and abundant silicon hydroxyl groups on the surface make it easy to realize [23–25].

Various species, including inorganic nanoparticles, organic molecules and biomolecules, have been selected as capping agents to enhance the dispersity of HMSNs. The linkages between capping agents and HMSNs, together with the capping agents, may endow HMSNs sensitivities to external stimuli, such as pH, redox, temperature and enzymes [26–30]. The tumor site shows different physical, chemical and biological properties from normal tissues, such as slightly acidic pH, slightly higher temperature and higher H2O2 concentration [31–34]. Smart HMSNs may intelligently distinguish the difference and achieve controlled release [35–38]. Poly (N-vinyl caprolactam) (PNVCL), a thermosensitive polymer with lower critical solution temperature (LCST) slightly lower than normal physiological temperature (T = 37 °C), is a commonly used capping agent to endow HMSNs thermosensitivity. Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent nanoparticles with spherical morphology and particle size less than 10 nm. Their excellent water solubility and moderate size present possibilities to be used as capping agents. pH sensitive linkers (Imine, acetal, hydrazone, etc.), which will fracture in slight acidic tumor site and result in intelligent release of drugs, are commonly used to tether HMSNs and capping agents.

As an endogenous gaseous biological mediator, nitric oxide (NO) is crucial in physiology and pathophysiology. NO has shown significance in tumor progression related to concentration [39]. Tumor growth can be accelerated at lower NO concentrations, while larger NO concentrations result in tumor cells apoptosis (>1 µM) [40]. In addition, co-delivery of NO and anticancer drugs can significantly enhance the antitumor effects. CDs with guanidine-rich surfaces can be used as endogenous NO donors, and release NO at high H2O2 concentration conditions of tumor site [41].

Herein, novel multiple sensitive HMSNs based DDSs with mixed shells (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs) were prepared, wherein CDs and PNVCL were the blend shells and acid-sensitive Schiff base bonds were between HMSNs and shells [42]. At room temperature, the PNVCL was soluble and the mesopores of the HMSNs were in open states. Therefore, anticancer drug-doxorubicin (DOX) could be transported freely through mesoporous pores. When the drug loaded DDSs (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX) were injected into the blood stream (pH = 7.4, T = 37 °C), PNVCL chains collapsed and the drugs were encapsulated inside. CDs acted as "caps" and their fine water solubility make the particles disperse well in blood instead of gathering. The acid-liable Schiff base bonds between the HMSNs and the shells endow the DDSs pH sensitivity, while the H2O2 sensitivity is given by CDs, which were prepared from arginine and rich in guanidine on surface [43]. At the slight acidic condition of tumor site, the Schiff base bonds break and the shells (CDs/PNVCL) fall off from HMSNs, resulting in opening of the pores and releasing of guest molecules. The high H2O2 concentration condition of tumor site lead to the release of NO [44–47]. Higher NO concentrations may result in tumor cells apoptosis ( Fig. 1). In sum, CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX were sensitive to temperature, pH, and H2O2 concentration. Drug loading could be successfully achieved and leakage in blood were restrain due to their thermosensitivity. Drug and NO were simultaneously released at acidic and high H2O2 concentration tumor site because of their pH and H2O2 sensitivity and they could both “kill” tumor cells. Moreover, the combination of drug and NO might have enhancive effects [48–50].

Glutaraldehyde (GA), L-arginine, ethylenediamine, anhydrous sodium carbonate (>99.8 %), mercaptoethylamine, cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), Dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, hydrochloric acid and dichloromethane were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl), phosphate-buffered solution (PBS: 0.01 mol L−1), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and other biological reagents were purchased from Accela ChemBio Co. Ltd (China). HeLa cervical cancer cells, MCF-7 breast cancer cells, Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Penicillin-Streptomycin (P/S) Solution, Catalase, Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) were purchased from Wuhan punuosai Life Technology Co., Ltd. A549 lung cancer cells and HEK293T human embryonic kidney cells were purchased from Shanghai Gaining Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

Arginine (1.0 g), ethylenediamine (0.345 g) and ultrapure water (15 mL) were mixed and dispersed using a simple ultrasonic process. The dispersion was irradiated by microwave (Midea Microwave M1-L202B, Foshan, China) for 4 min to give white powder, which was dispersed in 15 mL of ultrapure water and purified by dialysis for 12 h (MWCO: 1000 da). CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. The integrated path is shown in Scheme S2.

4 mL ammonium hydroxide, 20 mL ultrapure water and 142.8 mL ethanol were mixed via stirring for 30 min at 25 °C and TEOS solution (6 mL, 28 %) was added. Reaction for 2 h under stirring and subsequent centrifugation gave solid, which was washed with ethanol and ultrapure water three times, respectively and sSiO2 nanoparticles were obtained. A mixture of 10 mg sSiO2 and 20 mL ultrapure water was sonicated for 20 min to give sSiO2 suspension, which was mixed with CTAB/ultrapure water/ethanol/ammonia solution (150 mg/30 mL/30 mL/0.55 mL). After ultrasonic treatment for 2 h, CTAB was absorbed on the surface of sSiO2. After addition of TEOS (0.2 mL), the reaction proceeded under stirring for 6 h at 25 °C. Solid recovered by centrifugation was washed with ethanol and ultrapure water three times respectively to give SiO2 @CTAB-SiO2, which were dispersed in 10 mL Na2CO3 aqueous solution (45 mg mL−1). The reaction proceeded at 50 °C for 6 h under stirring. Centrifugation and drying gave crude HMSNs, which were immersed in a mixture of methanol/ ethanol/hydrochloric acid (30 mL/20 mL/4 mL) over 24 h at 60 °C. Centrifugation and washing with ultrapure water gave refined HMSNs.

Amino-functionalized HMSNs (HMSNs-NH2) were obtained by the reaction of HMSNs and APTES. APTES (150 mg) and HMSNs (100 mg) were added to a flask containing anhydrous toluene (50 mL) and dispersed by sonification. Refluxing at 70 °C for 12 h followed by centrifugation gave light pink powder. Ethanol was used to wash the solid three times. After vacuum drying, the purified HMSNs-NH2 were obtained. 1 mL glutaraldehyde and 4 mL ultrapure water were mixed and 100 mg HMSNs-NH2 were dispersed in the mixture, which was vigorously stirred at 25 °C for 4 h and centrifuged. Washing with ethanol three times and vacuum drying at 60 °C gave the desired aldehyde-functionalized HMSNs (HMSNs-CHO).

Mixing and sonification of HMSNs-CHO (50 mg) and ultrapure water (20 mL) gave dispersion. Under vigorous stirring, PNVCL-NH2 (150 mg) was added and the reaction proceeded for 4 h at 25 °C. The solid collected by centrifugation was purified by washing three times with ultrapure water and freeze drying to give PNVCL@HMSNs. Then the mixture of PNVCL@HMSNs/CDs/ultrapure water (50 mg/100 mg/20 mL) was dispersed well by sonification and stirred for 4 h at 25 °C. Centrifugation, washing with ultrapure water three times, and finally freeze-drying gave CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs. The preparation procedure is shown in Fig. S1.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the nanoparticles were collected by an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet 5700). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed using Hitachi H-7650 electron microscope (100 kV). The mesoporous structure, morphology and Zeta potential were investigated using TEM (JEOLJEM-2100, 200 kV) and Malvern Nano ZS. The pore diameter and the specific surface area were calculated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model, respectively. A SDT Q600 simultaneous DSC/DTA-TGA instrument was employed to record the thermogravimetric (TG) curves under nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in the temperature range of 25–800 °C. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC).

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured by a spectrofluorometer (FLS1000). The sample was uniformly distributed in the sample tank and the spectrum was captured by a multi-channel detector (all the recorded spectroscopic data were corrected for the spectral responses of both the spectrofluorometer and the integrating sphere). The absolute QY was calculated in real time.
A series of 200 µg mL−1 CDs aqueous solutions were prepared.

The sodium hydroxide solution (1.0 mol L−1) and hydrochloric acid solution (37%) were used to obtain solutions with pH from 1 to 14. The fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs solution at 385 nm under excitation at 340 nm was recorded. The fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs solution at neutral condition was denoted as P0 and those at different pH were denoted as P. A graph of P/P0 to pH was plotted to evaluate the stability.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs aqueous solutions at different temperatures were recorded. The fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs solution at 25 °C was denoted as P0 and those at different temperatures were denoted as P. A graph of P/P0 to temperature was plotted to evaluate the stability.

The released amount of NO in vitro was determined by Griess reagent method. Briefly, CDs and H2O2 aqueous solution (6.5, 12.5 and 25 mmol L−1) were dissolved in PBS (pH = 7.4, 90 mL). After a brief sonication, the mixture was incubated at 37 °C with slight stirring. At predetermined time intervals, a certain amount of solution (1.0 mL) was transferred to a centrifugal tube (5 mL). Then, ultrapure water (1.0 mL), 1 wt% sulfonamide (0.5 mL) and 0.1 wt% N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine hydrochloride solution (0.5 mL) were added to the centrifuge tube and reacted for 20 min. The absorbance of the mixture was recorded at 546 nm via a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General TU-1900). The same procedure was carried out in acetic acid buffer solution (pH = 5 and pH = 6.5) to study the release of NO at different pH.

The human cell lines used were A549, HeLa, MCF-7 and HEK293T and their origins were different. A549 lung cancer cells and MCF-7 breast cancer cells were from Chinese Academy of Sciences, HeLa cervical cancer cells were from domestic key laboratory and HEK293T human embryonic kidney cells were derived from Fudan cell bank. Cells were incubated in RPMI-1640 (A549, MCF-7) or DMEM medium (HeLa, HEK293T) with 10 % FBS and 1 % P/S in a water-saturated incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

The cells (A549 cells, HeLa cells or MCF-7 cells) were seeded in a 12-well plates with a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. After 12 h growth, 500 μL fresh medium containing 10 mg mL−1 particles (CDs, CDs@HMSNs or CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs) was added to the wells and the cultivation continued for another 4 h. Subsequently, DAF-FM (20 μL) was used as probes to treat with cells at 37 °C for 10 min and then excess DAF-FM was removed by washing with PBS three times. The cellular uptake experimental procedures of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX (containing DOX at a concentration of 10 µg mL−1) were the same, except that after the removal of excess DAF-FM, DAPI (20 μL) was added for nuclear staining for 6 min at 37 °C, followed by washing three times with PBS. Finally, an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Ts2-FL) was employed to observe the cells.

Cytotoxicities of CDs and HMSNs were investigated by HEK293T cells, while those of free DOX, HMSNs-DOX and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX were characterized by A549 cells. Cytotoxicities of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs were investigated by HEK293T cells and A549 cells.

The cells were incubated in 96-well dish with 100 μL fresh medium, and the inoculation density was 105 cells per well. After incubating for 12 h, the old medium was sucked away, and fresh medium (100 μL) was added. The cultivation continued for a while. Then each well was supplemented with 20 μL MTT (5.0 mg mL−1), and the culture continued for 4 h. Finally, MTT formazan crystals were dissolved by DMSO (150 μL) and the optical density (OD) of each well were detected at 492 nm via a microplate reader. Using DMEM substitute for RPMI-1640, the cytotoxicities against HEK293T cells were implemented with the same procedure. The cell viability (%) was got by the formula listed below:
Cell viability ( % )
=
(
OD
sample
−
OD
blank
OD
control
−
OD
blank
)
× 100 %

Calculation of the combination index (CI): CI = [IC50 (combined therapy)/IC50 (PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX alone)] + [IC50 (combined therapy)/IC50 (CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs alone)]. CI < 1 represents collaborative treatment, CI > 1 shows antagonism, and CI = 1 means additive effects.

Cytotoxicities of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX were characterized by HeLa and A549 cells in the presence of catalase. The activity of the catalase was 100,000 U g−1. Cells were incubated in 96-well plates at the density of 1 × 105 cells per well and cultured with 100 μL fresh medium. After 12 h of culture, the old medium was replaced with fresh medium (50 μL) containing catalase with enzyme activities of 400 U mL−1, 800 U mL−1, 1200 U mL−1, 1600 U mL−1 and 2000 U mL−1 [51]. After 1 h of incubation, fresh medium (50 μL) containing CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX (containing DOX at a concentration 20 µg mL−1) was added and the incubation continued for another 24 h. A incubation at 37 °C was performed for 4 h after MTT (20 μL, 5.0 mg mL−1) was added to each template and then the medium was removed and washed with PBS. 150 μL DMSO was added to dissolve MTT formazan and the absorbance values of the solution were detected at 492 nm by a microplate reader.
All tests were repeated at least 3 times, and six multiple wells were set for each concentration for analysis and comparison.

The cytotoxicity of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX was further studied using Calcein-AM/PI co-staining method. HeLa cells were seeded in a 24-well plate with a density of 1 × 105 and incubated for 12 h. Then the old medium was replaced with fresh medium containing PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX (containing DOX at a concentration of 50 µg mL−1) and the incubation for another 4 h proceeded. PBS was used as a negative control. Then each hole was washed 3 times with PBS (1 mL), and the cells were stained with a mixed Calcein-AM/PI solution for 30 min at 37 °C in a dark place. Finally, live cells (green) and dead cells (red) were imaged under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Ts2-FL).

The data were statistically analyzed using graphpad prism version 9.4.0 (graphpad Software Inc., CA, USA). All data were presented as mean ± SD (standard deviation). The significance of the experimental data was assessed via two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). ***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01 and *p < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.
The amino-terminated PNVCL (PNVCL-NH2) was prepared according to the previously reported method [52] and the details are in the Supporting information.

CDs were prepared by microwave irradiation. The fluorescence QY of CDs was 14.24 %. TEM and DLS (Fig. S3A, B) showed that CDs were spheres with diameters of about 4 nm. As shown in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3C), the peaks at 1625 cm−1, 1188 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 were the characteristic peaks of guanidine groups, suggesting that there were abundant guanidine groups on the surface of CDs. Fig. S3D indicated that the dilute CDs solution exhibited blue fluorescence under the optimum excitation wavelength (340 nm). The fluorescence stabilities of CDs at different pH and temperature were evaluated. There were no significant fluctuations for P/P0 within the pH range of 4–12 and the temperature range of 4–70 °C (Fig. S4), demonstrating that the fluorescence properties of CDs were stable under physiological conditions.

HMSNs were prepared by selective etching [53]. SEM and TEM images ( Fig. 2A and B) demonstrated that the prepared HMSNs displayed well-dispersed hollow spheres morphology and mesoporous network shells with diameters of approximately 400 nm. The DLS (Fig. 2D) demonstrated that the average diameter of the particles was 400 nm, which was consistent with those of SEM and TEM and also indicated that the dispersion was stable. The characteristic peaks in Fig. S5 confirmed the structure of the HMSNs [54–56]. The zeta potential value was negative due to the abundant silicon hydroxyl groups on the surface (Fig. S6). HMSNs displayed a typical IV N2 adsorption desorption isotherm, which indicated there are many mesoporous (Fig. S7A). The surface area and the pore volume were 830 m2 g−1 and 0.26 cc g−1 respectively (Fig. S7A, B). Bare HMSNs exhibited a weight loss of 11.95 %, which was attributed to humidity and remained solvent molecules (Fig. S8).

HMSNs-NH2 were obtained by the reaction of APTES and HMSNs. The peak at 3380 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1 belonged to N–H vibration (Fig. S5). The signal at 960 cm−1 (Si-OH) was abated in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S5) and the zeta potential (Fig. S6) value became positive since some silicon hydroxyl groups were substituted by amino groups. The reaction of HMSNs-NH2 and glutaraldehyde gave HMSNs-CHO. The characteristic absorption peaks of OC-H (2928 cm−1) and CO (1466 cm−1) in FTIR spectrum (Fig. S5) indicated that amino groups were replaced by aldehyde groups, which was also demonstrated by the larger positive zeta potential value (Fig. S6) of HMSNs-CHO. Schiff base reaction between PNVCL-NH2 and HMSNs-CHO gave PNVCL@HMSNs. After grafting with PNVCL, the surface area decreased from 830 m2 g−1 to 67.55 m2 g−1 and the pore volume decreased from 0.26 cc g−1 to 0.19 cc g−1 (Fig. S7A, B). The weight loss of PNVCL@HMSNs increased from 11.95 % to 30.93 % (Fig. S8), and the zeta potential changed (Fig. S6), indicating that the grafting of PNVCL was successful.

CDs were further grafted via Schiff base reaction to obtain CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs. The characteristic peak intensity at 2928 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs decreased significantly (Fig. S5) and the zeta potential changed (Fig. S6), indicating that most aldehyde groups disappeared. The surfaces of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs (Fig. 2C) were much rougher than those of HMSNs (Fig. 2A). After grafting, their specific surface area and pore volume decreased to 26.49 m2 g−1 and 0.089 cc g−1, respectively (Fig. S7A, B). The weight loss increased from 30.93 % to 34.75 % due to the modification (Fig. S8).

The drug encapsulation and delivery behavior were investigated using DOX as a model drug. Drug loading capacity of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX was 25.94 % and the drug loading efficiency was 58.38%. At neutral conditions, the cumulative release amount at 37 °C was by far slower than that at 25 °C ( Fig. 3A). The thermosensitive release behavior was endowed by PNVCL chain and could avoid the drug leakage in blood and normal tissue. At pH 5.0, the cumulative release amount in 32 h was 75.25%, which was 1.45 times higher than that at pH 6.5 (51.58 %) and 4.13 times higher than that at pH 7.4 (18.23 %) (Fig. 3B). The larger cumulative release amount in acidic condition came from two reason: the larger solubility of cationic drugs DOX and fracture of Schiff base bond. The pH dependent release properties demonstrated that the burst release could be triggered at the slight acidic condition of tumor site [57,58].

CDs with guanidine-rich surfaces could be used as endogenous NO donors, which should release NO in presence of H2O2. The cumulative release amount of NO increased with the H2O2 concentration as shown in Fig. 4A and had no relationship with pH value (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake and toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: CDs, CDs@HMSNs and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs were cultured with tumor cells (HeLa cells, A549 cells or MCF-7 cells) respectively for 4 h and the fluorescence inverted microscope photographs of HeLa cells were showed in Fig. 5. The first column was the signals of NO with DAF-FM DA as a fluorescent probe and the difference in the fluorescence intensity of the three groups was negligible. The overlap of the signals of NO and CDs (column three) demonstrated that NO was released by CDs inside the tumor cells. The same results were observed in the fluorescence inverted microscope photographs of A549 cells and MCF-7 cells (Fig. S9). CDs, CDs@HMSNs and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs could internalize into tumor cells and released NO inside the cells. The release amount of NO was independent of grafting.

In vitro cellular uptake of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX were instigated using HeLa cells ( Fig. 6) or A549 cells (Fig. S10). After 2 h of incubation with tumor cells, the weak fluorescence signals of CDs, DOX, and NO could be seen. With the extension of incubation time, the fluorescence intensities became stronger and the cells shrank and became round.

Cytotoxicity was detected by MTT method. After treatment with HMSNs, CDs, and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs for 48 h, the cell viability of normal cells (HEK293T) exceeded 80% (Fig. S11). HMSNs, CDs and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs showed good biocompatibility and safety to normal cells (HEK293T) due to small amount of NO was released in low H2O2 concentration surrounding. They were of low toxicity.

CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs showed a certain inhibitory effect on tumor cells (A549 cells), and the effect increased with incubation time (Fig. S12A). CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs released NO in high H2O2 concentration surrounding of tumor cells and resulted in cell apoptosis without DOX, indicating CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs themselves possessed an anticancer effect due to the CDs on the surface.

PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX and HMSNs-DOX both showed definite cytotoxicity as free DOX, which could kill cells. The cytotoxicity of PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX was slightly lower than those of HMSNs-DOX and free DOX due to their controlled release character (Fig. S12B).

MTT and Calcein-AM/PI co-staining assay were both used to evaluated the cytotoxicity of PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX. In MTT assay, tumor cells (HeLa and A549 cells) were selected to evaluated the cytotoxicity of PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX and CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX. Two carries both showed certain inhibitory effects on cancer cells and the effects enhanced with the concentration of carries. CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX showed more significant cytotoxicity than PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX since they release NO and DOX simultaneously at tumor site, which both had anticancer effect, and the two together might have synergistic effects ( Fig. 7A, B). IC50 of the nanoparticles were list in Table S1 and the combination index (CI) calculated was 0.441 (less than 1) [59], which confirmed the synergistic effects of the CDs and DOX. The results (Fig. 7C) of Calcein-AM/PI co-staining assay were consistent with that of MTT assay.

MTT assay of CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX in the presence of catalase showed that the cell viability increased significantly and the effects increased with the activity of catalase (Fig. 7D, E). The viability of tumor cells increased with the addition of catalase, which further demonstrated that the release of NO was triggered by the high H2O2 concentration surrounding of tumor cells.

Multi-responsive drug delivery vehicles, which integrated drug delivery and the release of NO, were designed and synthesized successfully. For CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX, the drug loading capacity and drug loading efficiency were up to 25.94 % and 58.38 %, respectively. The vehicles showed intelligent release properties. Meanwhile, CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX could release NO under the environment of high H2O2 concentration at the tumor site. In sum, the multi-responsive CDs/PNVCL@HMSNs-DOX could release NO and DOX simultaneously in tumor environment, which could improve the treatment effect.

Shu Wu：Investigation, Data Curation, Writing – Original Draft Jinjing Shi: Methodology Xia Chen: Supervision Lu Bai: Validation, Formal analysis Qiuhua Wu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing Guolin Zhang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJC201906) and Start-up Funds for Doctoral Research of Liaoning University (a280008030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113346.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
